topic: Mughal Empire — Babur, Humayun, Sher Shah & Akbar

questions.text: The First Battle of Panipat (1526 CE) was fought between Babur and:
questions.explanation: The First Battle of Panipat (April 21, 1526) was between Babur (Timurid, ruler of Kabul) and Ibrahim Lodi (last Sultan of Delhi). Babur won using artillery and the tulughma flanking tactic, killing Ibrahim Lodi and ending the Delhi Sultanate. The Mughal Empire began with this battle.

questions.text: Sher Shah Suri introduced which famous currency that is still the name of India's currency?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah Suri introduced the rupiya (silver coin of fixed weight and purity) which became the standard currency. The modern Indian Rupee (₹) traces its lineage to Sher Shah's rupiya. He also issued the dam (copper) and muhar (gold) coins.

questions.text: Akbar's revenue system — the 'Ain-i-Dahsala' — was designed by which minister?
questions.explanation: The Ain-i-Dahsala (10-year revenue system) was designed by Raja Todar Mal — Akbar's finance minister. It measured land, classified it by fertility, and used 10-year averages of yield and prices to set fair tax rates. It is considered the most scientific pre-modern land revenue system in India.

Akbar introduced the Mansabdari System — a unified civil-military hierarchy where all officers held a mansab (rank) with two components: zat (personal salary rank) and sawar (cavalry contingent). Ranks ranged from 10 to 10,000. The system was not hereditary — all appointments were by the Emperor.

questions.text: Humayun was defeated by Sher Shah at the Battle of Kanauj in 1540 CE and then spent 15 years in exile. Where was he born (Akbar born)?
questions.explanation: Akbar was born on October 15, 1542 CE at Amarkot (Umarkot, Sindh, now Pakistan) while Humayun was in exile, seeking refuge with the local Rajput chief Rana Prasad. Humayun then proceeded to the Safavid court in Iran. Akbar was left behind with his uncle Kamran for some time.

questions.text: The 'Buland Darwaza' at Fatehpur Sikri was built to commemorate Akbar's conquest of which territory?
questions.explanation: Buland Darwaza (52.4 m — highest gateway in Asia) was built in 1575 CE to commemorate Akbar's conquest of Gujarat (1572–73). The inscription on the gateway reads: 'Isa son of Mary said: The world is a bridge, pass over it but do not build your houses upon it. He who hopes for an hour may hope for eternity.' Akbar built it to mark this major military victory.

questions.text: The Battle of Haldighati (1576 CE) was fought between Akbar's forces and:
questions.explanation: The Battle of Haldighati (June 1576) was between Akbar's forces (led by Raja Man Singh of Amber) and Rana Pratap Singh of Mewar. Rana Pratap escaped on his legendary horse Chetak and continued guerrilla warfare. He never surrendered to the Mughals. Mewar was the only major Rajput kingdom that refused submission.

questions.text: The Grand Trunk Road (GTR) built by Sher Shah Suri ran from:
questions.explanation: Sher Shah's GTR ran from Sonargaon (near Dhaka, Bengal) to Peshawar (~2,400 km), with sarais (rest houses) every 2 kos, trees for shade, and a horse relay postal system. It connected Bengal to the NW Frontier. The British extended it as the Grand Trunk Road — it is now approximately NH-44 in India.

questions.text: Babur's autobiography 'Baburnama' was written in which language?
questions.explanation: Babur wrote the Baburnama (also called Tuzuk-i-Baburi) in Chagatai Turkic — his mother tongue. It was later translated into Persian during Akbar's reign by Abdur Rahim Khan-i-Khana. It is one of the earliest true autobiographies in Asiatic literature and includes vivid descriptions of nature, battles, grief, and joy.

questions.text: The Ibadat Khana (House of Worship) was built by Akbar at Fatehpur Sikri for what purpose?
options: As a personal prayer room for the Emperor
For religious debates among scholars of all faiths
As a treasury for storing religious texts
As a mosque for the court Muslims
questions.explanation: Ibadat Khana (built 1575 CE) was built for inter-faith religious discussions. Initially only Muslim scholars debated there; Akbar later opened it to Hindus, Zoroastrians, Jains, and Christians (Portuguese Jesuits). These debates influenced his thinking toward the syncretic Din-i-Ilahi (1582).

questions.text: Who was Abul Fazl, and what did he write?
options: Akbar's general; wrote Ain-i-Akbari as military manual
Akbar's chief minister and court historian; wrote Akbarnama (biography) and Ain-i-Akbari (administrative manual)
Akbar's treasurer; compiled the revenue records of the empire
A Jesuit priest who described Akbar's court in his letters
questions.explanation: Abul Fazl was Akbar's chief minister and court chronicler. He wrote: (1) Akbarnama — a 3-volume biography of Akbar covering Babur and Humayun too; (2) Ain-i-Akbari — the third volume of Akbarnama, a detailed administrative/statistical survey of the empire. He was killed in 1602 by Bir Singh Bundela on Prince Salim's orders.

questions.text: The Second Battle of Panipat (1556) was fought between Akbar and which opponent?
questions.explanation: The Second Battle of Panipat (November 5, 1556) was between Akbar (with regent Bairam Khan) and Hemu — a Hindu general who had been serving the Sur Afghan dynasty and had captured Delhi. Hemu was wounded by an arrow during the battle, captured, and executed. Akbar secured Delhi with this victory.

questions.text: Tansen, the greatest musician of the Mughal era, was one of Akbar's Navratnas. He was originally in the court of which ruler before Akbar?
questions.explanation: Tansen (Miyan Tansen) was originally in the court of Raja Ramchandra of Rewa (Madhya Pradesh). Akbar heard of his talent and invited him — Akbar reportedly waited years before Tansen agreed to join his court. Tansen is associated with the Dhrupad style of Hindustani classical music and is buried in Gwalior.

questions.text: Fatehpur Sikri was built near a site associated with which Sufi saint whose blessings preceded the birth of Akbar's heir?
questions.explanation: Akbar built Fatehpur Sikri (~1571–1585) near the hermitage of Sheikh Salim Chishti (Chishti order), who had predicted/blessed the birth of Akbar's heir Salim (later Jahangir). In gratitude, Akbar built his new capital there. The white marble tomb of Sheikh Salim Chishti within Fatehpur Sikri is still visited by devotees seeking children.

questions.text: Babur won the Battle of Khanwa (1527) against Rana Sanga. Before this battle, to motivate his troops, Babur:
options: Promised each soldier a share of Rajput gold
Declared jihad and gave up wine (a significant personal sacrifice)
Received a blessing from the Caliph of Baghdad
Built a temporary mosque on the battlefield
questions.explanation: Before the Battle of Khanwa, Babur gave a famous speech declaring jihad — and to prove his sincerity, he publicly destroyed his wine vessels and gave up drinking (he was known for his love of wine). This was a significant personal sacrifice and a political act to rally his Muslim troops. He won the battle and titled himself 'Ghazi'.

questions.text: Sher Shah died while conducting a siege of which fort?
questions.explanation: Sher Shah Suri died on May 22, 1545 CE during the siege of Kalinjar Fort (Bundelkhand, Madhya Pradesh) — killed by an accidental gunpowder explosion when a shell hit a supply of gunpowder. He died at the height of his power, having transformed India's administration. His empire fell apart within a decade.
